body_excerpt:
### Suggested enhancement

The macros `MBEDTLS_PKCS1_V15` and `MBEDTLS_PKCS1_V21` are internal crypto macros, which we should no longer be using on the Mbed TLS side.

Originally, each of these macros might mean either encryption or signature. Fortunately, X.509 uses only signatures, and these days so does TLS (we've removed the TLS 1.2 key exchanges based on RSA encryption in Mbed TLS 4.0: [RSA-PSK](https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/issues/9681) and [RSA](https://github.com/Mbed-TLS/mbedtls/issues/9682)).

So, this should be as simple as:
- `s/MBEDTLS_PKCS1_V15/PSA_WANT_ALG_RSA_PKCS1V15_SIGN/`
- `s/MBEDTLS_PKCS1_V21/PSA_WANT_ALG_RSA_PSS/`

Well, except that PSA has variants for these algs:
- `PSA_WANT_ALG_RSA_PKCS1V15_SIGN` vs `PSA_WANT_ALG_RSA_PKCS1V15_SIGN_RAW`
- `PSA_WANT_ALG_RSA_PSS` vs `PSA_WANT_ALG_RSA_PSS_ANY_SALT`

So perhaps we first need to have some abstraction macros such as `PSA_HAVE_RSA_PKCS1V15_SIGN_ANY` and `PSA_HAVE_RSA_PSS_ANY`? A bit like `PSA_HAVE_ALG_ECDSA_SIGN` which means we can create either randomized or deterministic ECDSA signatures. (If we add those, should they live on the crypto side or the mbedtls side?)

And then it's a simple substitution from the legacy macros to these new light abstraction macros.

### Justification

Mbed TLS needs this because using crypto-internal feature macros is a thing of the past.
title: Quit using `MBEDTLS_PKCS1_Vxx` macros

body_excerpt:
The ssl-opt.sh tests `DTLS proxy: 3d, openssl client, fragmentation`, `DTLS proxy: 3d, openssl client, fragmentation, nbio`, `DTLS proxy: 3d, gnutls client, fragmentation` and `DTLS proxy: 3d, gnutls client, fragmentation, nbio=2` fail intermittently on the CI with a frequency that  significantly impacts CI throughput. Thus we are going to disable them.

These test cases were introduced as part of the DTLS ClientHello reassembly work: #10576 (development) and #10623 (3.6).

This issue is to understand the intermittent failures and how we could re-enable the test cases.

We should also consider doing deterministic tests in `test_suite_ssl` of DTLS fragment re-ordering.
title: Investigate "DTLS Proxy: 3d, (gnutls|openssl), fragmentation" intermittent failures

body_excerpt:
## Summary

The implementation does not fully enforce the TLS 1.3 requirement that, when the server is able to provide a certificate chain whose certificates are signed with algorithms advertised by the client, it should select and send such a compliant chain.  
Static analysis shows that the certificate selection logic only matches the client’s advertised **CertificateVerify** signature schemes and key type compatibility, but does not evaluate the signature hash algorithms used across the configured certificate chain. Runtime testing further confirms this gap: even when both a SHA-1-signed chain and a SHA-256-signed chain are simultaneously available, the server may still select the SHA-1-signed chain despite the client advertising only SHA-2 signature schemes.  
This creates a reproducible standards-conformance issue with practical interoperability, observability, and weak-chain exposure implications.

## 1. Requirement (RFC 8446 / TLS1.3.txt)

Specification file: `D:\project\conditionFuzzing\document\TLS1.3.txt`

Relevant original text:

```text
3726:    All certificates provided by the server MUST be signed by a signature
3727:    algorithm advertised by the client if it is able to provide such a
3728:    chain (see Section 4.2.3).  Certificates that are self-signed or
3729:    certificates that are expected to be trust anchors are not validated
3730:    as part of the chain and therefore MAY be signed with any algorithm.
3731:
3732:    If the server cannot produce a cert
title: Comprehensive Analysis: TLS 1.3 Certificate Signature Algorithm Constraint

body_excerpt:
### Suggested enhancement
RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3 specification), Section 5.1, states:

Handshake messages MAY be coalesced into a single TLSPlaintext record
or fragmented across several records, provided that:
- ...
-Handshake messages MUST NOT span key changes. Implementations
MUST verify that all messages immediately preceding a key change
align with a record boundary; if not, then they MUST terminate the
connection with an "unexpected_message" alert. Because the
ClientHello, EndOfEarlyData, ServerHello, Finished, and KeyUpdate
messages can immediately precede a key change, implementations
MUST send these messages in alignment with a record boundary.

Currently, we do not "verify that all messages immediately preceding a key change
align with a record boundary".

This issue is to investigate how to verify that all messages immediately preceding a key change
align with a record boundary.

### Justification

Mbed TLS needs this to comply with RFC 8446.
title: TLS 1.3: Enforce that handshake messages do not span key changes

body_excerpt:
# mbedTLS TLS 1.3 weak-hash certificate alert mapping inconsistency

## 1. Problem Summary

- SHA-1 case: mbedTLS rejects certificates that require SHA-1 for validation, but the alert sent is not consistently `bad_certificate(42)`.
- MD5 case: mbedTLS rejects certificates that require MD5 for validation, but the alert sent is not consistently `bad_certificate(42)`.

In both cases the handshake fails (correct), but the alert code observed at runtime is `certificate_unknown(46)` rather than `bad_certificate(42)`.

## 2. RFC 8446 Source Text

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.4.2.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.4.2.2):

```text
Any endpoint receiving any certificate which it would need to
validate using any signature algorithm using an MD5 hash MUST abort
the handshake with a "bad_certificate" alert.  SHA-1 is deprecated,
and it is RECOMMENDED that any endpoint receiving any certificate
which it would need to validate using any signature algorithm using a
SHA-1 hash abort the handshake with a "bad_certificate" alert.
```

Alert code definitions from [RFC 8446, Section 6.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-6.2):

- `bad_certificate(42)`: a certificate was corrupt, contained signatures that did not verify correctly, etc.
- `certificate_unknown(46)`: some other (unspecified) issue arose in processing the certificate.

## 3. Code Description (mbedTLS)

### 3.1 Weak-hash certificates are rejected at the X.509 layer

The default verification profile
title: mbedTLS TLS 1.3 weak-hash certificate alert mapping inconsistency

body_excerpt:
# mbedTLS TLS 1.3 X.509 `cert_data` length boundary inconsistency

## 1. Summary

RFC 8446 defines the length range for X.509 `cert_data` as `1..2^24-1`. The mbedTLS implementation enforces a narrower range: `128 <= cert_data_len < 64K`. Both the lower bound and the upper bound differ from the standard.

## 2. RFC 8446 Source Text

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.4.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.4.2):

```text
case X509:
  opaque cert_data<1..2^24-1>;
```

RFC allows:
- minimum length: `1`
- maximum length: `2^24 - 1`

## 3. Code Description

From `library/ssl_tls13_generic.c`:

```c
/* In theory, the CRT can be up to 2^24 Bytes, but we don't support
 * anything beyond 2^16 = 64K. Otherwise as in the TLS 1.2 code,
 * check that we have a minimum of 128 bytes of data, this is not
 * clear why we need that though.
 */
if ((cert_data_len < 128) || (cert_data_len >= 0x10000)) {
    MBEDTLS_SSL_DEBUG_MSG(1, ("bad Certificate message"));
    MBEDTLS_SSL_PEND_FATAL_ALERT(MBEDTLS_SSL_ALERT_MSG_DECODE_ERROR,
                                 MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_DECODE_ERROR);
    return MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_DECODE_ERROR;
}
```

mbedTLS enforces:
- lower bound: `cert_data_len >= 128`
- upper bound: `cert_data_len < 0x10000` (i.e., `< 64K`)

## 4. Why This Is Inconsistent

| Bound | RFC 8446 | mbedTLS |
|-------|----------|---------|
| Lower | `1` | `128` |
| Upper | `2^24 - 1` | `< 64K` |

The implementation accepts a significantly narrower range than the standard permit
title: mbedTLS TLS 1.3 X.509 `cert_data` length boundary inconsistency

body_excerpt:
# Empty Client Certificate Uses `no_certificate` Instead of `certificate_required`

## 1. Summary

The core issue is not that mbedTLS fails to detect the missing client certificate. Rather:

- mbedTLS **does** correctly detect the case where the client sends an empty `Certificate` message after being requested to authenticate.
- But when the server aborts, it sends `no_certificate(41)` instead of the TLS 1.3-mandated `certificate_required(116)`.

Both alert constants are defined in the codebase. The inconsistency is purely in the alert-code selection for this specific TLS 1.3 condition.

Therefore this item is classified as **Not Satisfied** with respect to alert semantics: the failure is detected correctly, but the alert mapping does not match the RFC requirement.

## 2. Specification Description

### 2.1 Relevant TLS 1.3 requirement

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.4.2.1](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.4.2.1):

```text
   If the client does not send any certificates (i.e., it sends an empty
   Certificate message), the server MAY at its discretion either
   continue the handshake without client authentication or abort the
   handshake with a "certificate_required" alert.
```

### 2.2 Alert constant in the specification

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 6.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-6.2):

```text
   certificate_required(116)
```

## 3. Code Description

### 3.1 Empty peer certificate handling in TLS 1.3 certificate processing

Fr
title: Empty Client Certificate Uses `no_certificate` Instead of `certificate_required`

body_excerpt:
# TLS 1.3 CCS Compatibility Handling Deviates from RFC 8446

## 1. Summary

The core issue is not that mbedTLS ignores the CCS compatibility rule entirely. Rather:

- mbedTLS **does** implement the basic dummy-CCS format checks: payload length must be 1 and the byte must be `0x01`.
- But the implementation adopts a simplified compatibility strategy that loses two distinctions required by RFC 8446:
  - **CCS time-window (Partially Satisfied):** The RFC allows a dummy CCS to be silently dropped only within the window from the first `ClientHello` to the peer's `Finished`. mbedTLS validates format but does not explicitly enforce this time-window restriction.
  - **Protected CCS rejection (Not Satisfied):** The RFC requires that a *protected* `change_cipher_spec` record `MUST` abort with `unexpected_message`. No dedicated rejection branch for protected CCS was found in the inspected receive path.

Therefore the two items are classified separately: the time-window item as **Partially Satisfied** and the protected-CCS item as **Not Satisfied**.

## 2. Specification Description

### 2.1 Relevant TLS 1.3 requirement

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Appendix D.4](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#appendix-D.4):

```text
   An implementation may receive an unencrypted record of type
   change_cipher_spec consisting of the single byte value 0x01 at any
   time after the first ClientHello message has been sent or received
   and before the peer's Finished message has been received and MUST
title: TLS 1.3 CCS Compatibility Handling Deviates from RFC 8446

body_excerpt:
# TLS 1.3 KeyShare Ordering Semantics Are Only Partially Covered

## 1. Summary

The core issue is not that mbedTLS ignores the `supported_groups` ordering requirement entirely. Rather:

- mbedTLS **does** validate that each offered `KeyShareEntry` corresponds to a group in `supported_groups` (via `mbedtls_ssl_named_group_is_offered`).
- But the client is designed to send **only one** `KeyShareEntry`, so the RFC's `MUST appear in the same order` rule — which is conditional on multiple shares being present — is never exercised.
- On the receive side, no explicit ordering check was found that compares the relative order of incoming `client_shares` against `supported_groups`.

Therefore this item is classified as **Partially Satisfied**: the single-share common path does not violate the rule, but the full multi-share ordering semantics are not implemented.

## 2. Specification Description

### 2.1 Relevant TLS 1.3 requirement

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2.8](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.8):

```text
   client_shares:  A list of offered KeyShareEntry values in descending
      order of client preference.

   This vector MAY be empty if the client is requesting a
   HelloRetryRequest.  Each KeyShareEntry value MUST correspond to a
   group offered in the "supported_groups" extension and MUST appear in
   the same order.  However, the values MAY be a non-contiguous subset
   of the "supported_groups" extension and MAY omit the most preferred
   groups
title: TLS 1.3 KeyShare Ordering Semantics Are Only Partially Covered

body_excerpt:
# TLS 1.3 Cookie Minimum-Length Check Gap

## 1. Summary

The core issue is not that mbedTLS lacks a cookie parsing path. Rather:

- mbedTLS **does** implement TLS 1.3 cookie parsing: it reads the 16-bit length, checks buffer bounds, and stores the cookie for later echo in the second `ClientHello`.
- But it does **not** explicitly enforce the RFC lower bound `cookie<1..2^16-1>`, meaning a zero-length cookie is accepted and stored without error.
- A targeted probe confirmed this: after injecting a zero-length cookie in a `HelloRetryRequest`, the client stored `cookie_len == 0` and the test passed.

Therefore this item is classified as **Partially Satisfied**: the parsing infrastructure is present, but the RFC-defined minimum-length constraint is not enforced.

## 2. Specification Description

### 2.1 Cookie structure and minimum length

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.2):

```text
      struct {
          opaque cookie<1..2^16-1>;
      } Cookie;
```

This means that the TLS 1.3 `cookie` field:

- must have minimum length `1`
- may have maximum length `2^16 - 1`

So a zero-length cookie is outside the specified range.

### 2.2 Client behavior when a cookie is received in HRR

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.2):

```text
   When sending a HelloRetryRequest, the server MAY provide a "cookie"
   extension to the client (this is an exception to the usual rule tha
title: TLS 1.3 Cookie Minimum-Length Check Gap

body_excerpt:
# Duplicate Extension-Type Uniqueness Is Not Enforced as a Unified Hard-Fail Rule

## 1. Summary

The core issue is not that mbedTLS never rejects a duplicate extension. Rather:

- mbedTLS **does** enforce some extension rules: it rejects extensions not allowed in the current message, and it has a special placement check for `pre_shared_key`.
- But there is **no unified, explicit duplicate-type hard-fail check** in the generic extension checker — the bitmask is updated with `|=` (idempotent), so a repeated extension type is silently absorbed rather than rejected.
- In practice, a duplicate-extension probe still fails, but with `MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_INVALID_MAC` (late transcript/key mismatch) rather than an immediate duplicate-rule rejection.

Therefore this item is classified as **Not Satisfied**: the [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2) `MUST NOT` uniqueness rule is not enforced at the extension-parsing layer.

## 2. Specification Description

### 2.1 Relevant TLS 1.3 requirement

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2):

```text
   When multiple extensions of different types are present, the
   extensions MAY appear in any order, with the exception of
   "pre_shared_key" (Section 4.2.11) which MUST be the last extension in
   the ClientHello (but can appear anywhere in the ServerHello
   extensions block).  There MUST NOT be more than one extension of the
   same type in a given e
title: Duplicate Extension-Type Uniqueness Is Not Enforced as a Unified Hard-Fail Rule

body_excerpt:
# mbedTLS TLS 1.3 `certificate_authorities` Validation Gap

## 1. Problem Summary

In the TLS 1.3 `CertificateRequest` parsing path, mbedTLS accepts the `certificate_authorities` extension type, but does not implement dedicated parsing of its internal structure. As a result, the client does not explicitly validate:

- whether the `authorities` vector satisfies `3..2^16-1`
- whether each `DistinguishedName` satisfies `1..2^16-1`

In practice, malformed `certificate_authorities` contents can be received and ignored, instead of being rejected during handshake parsing.

## 2. RFC 8446 Source Text

From [RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), Section 4.2.4](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-4.2.4):

```text
4.2.4.  Certificate Authorities

   The "certificate_authorities" extension is used to indicate the
   certificate authorities (CAs) which an endpoint supports and which
   SHOULD be used by the receiving endpoint to guide certificate
   selection.

   The body of the "certificate_authorities" extension consists of a
   CertificateAuthoritiesExtension structure.

      opaque DistinguishedName<1..2^16-1>;

      struct {
          DistinguishedName authorities<3..2^16-1>;
      } CertificateAuthoritiesExtension;

   authorities:  A list of the distinguished names [X501] of acceptable
   certificate authorities, represented in DER-encoded [X690] format.
```

## 3. Code Description

### 3.1 `certificate_authorities` is allowed in `CertificateRequest`

From `library/ssl_misc.h`:

```c
/
title: mbedTLS TLS 1.3 `certificate_authorities` Validation Gap

body_excerpt:
In the TLS-Exporter for TLS 1.3 we mistakenly call PSA_HASH_LENGTH() on an mbedtls_md_type_t when it should be called on a psa_algorithm_t.

Fortunately, these two types have almost the same values, since we have previously aligned them to make conversion more efficient. As a result, PSA_HASH_LENGTH() produces exactly the same value when called on an mbedtls_md_type_t as with the equivalent psa_algorithm_t.

Thanks to this happy coincidence, fix a largely cosmetic issue (rather than a major functional bug).

## PR checklist

Please remove the segment/s on either side of the | symbol as appropriate, and add any relevant link/s to the end of the line.
If the provided content is part of the present PR remove the # symbol.

- [x] **changelog** not required because: No user-facing change
- [x] **development PR** here
- [x] **TF-PSA-Crypto PR** not required because: TLS only
- [x] **framework PR** not required
- [x] **3.6 PR** provided #10602 
- **tests** not required because: Existing tests suffice
title: Fix missing type conversion in the TLS-Exporter

In the TLS-Exporter for TLS 1.3 we mistakenly call PSA_HASH_LENGTH() on an mbedtls_md_type_t when it should be called on a psa_algorithm_t.

Fortunately, these two types have almost the same values, since we have previously aligned them to make conversion more efficient. As a result, PSA_HASH_LENGTH() produces exactly the same value when called on an mbedtls_md_type_t as with the equivalent psa_algorithm_t.

Thanks to this happy coincidence, fix a largely cosmetic issue (rather than a major functional bug).

body_excerpt:
## Description

Parse and store all certificate policy OIDs instead of rejecting non-anyPolicy OIDs with MBEDTLS_ERR_X509_FEATURE_UNAVAILABLE. Per RFC 5280 section 4.2.1.4, certificate policy enforcement is optional and should be left to the application. This aligns Mbed TLS behavior with OpenSSL, GnuTLS, and wolfSSL, which all accept certificates with custom/enterprise policy OIDs. Unknown policy OIDs are now rendered as numeric dotted strings (e.g. 1.2.3.4) in certificate info output.
                                                                                                                                                                       
Fixes #8123. Related to #8935, #4062, #7182.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            

## PR checklist

  - changelog provided
  - development PR provided # | not required because: this is the development PR
  - TF-PSA-Crypto PR not required because: no changes to TF-PSA-Crypto
  - framework PR not required
  - 3.6 PR not required because: should be discussed with maintainers for backport (v3.6.5 backport available here as reference https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/mbedtls/pull/80)
  - tests provided

### Notes

---
title: x509: Parse all certificate policy OIDs per RFC 5280

body_excerpt:
## Description

TLS handshake messages such as Certificate can exceed `MBEDTLS_SSL_IN_CONTENT_LEN` (16KB) when servers use certificate chains with many intermediate CAs or certificates with many SANs. The existing handshake defragmentation reassembles fragmented messages in-place, but is limited to the compile-time buffer size — connections fail with "requesting more data than fits" when the reassembled message exceeds the buffer.

This PR adds a new compile-time option `MBEDTLS_SSL_TLS_HS_LARGE_MSG` that extends the existing in-place reassembly with dynamic buffer resizing. When enabled and configured at runtime via `mbedtls_ssl_conf_max_handshake_msg_len()`, the input buffer is proactively enlarged at the initial handshake fragment if the message will not fit.

Related to #1840 (this addresses the case where the reassembled handshake message exceeds the buffer size; the basic fragmentation support was added in PR #9872).

### Design

- Extends the existing in-place reassembly — no separate buffer or new reassembly path
- Buffer is resized at the initial fragment, before the reassembly loop begins
- Allocates to the configured max once; the enlarged buffer remains for the lifetime of the SSL context
- Only triggers when the message exceeds `MBEDTLS_SSL_IN_CONTENT_LEN` — no cost for normal-sized messages
- TLS-only (not DTLS)
- This feature serves the opposite purpose of `MBEDTLS_SSL_VARIABLE_BUFFER_LENGTH` (which shrinks buffers); they should be considered mut
title: Add MBEDTLS_SSL_TLS_HS_LARGE_MSG for large handshake message reassembly

body_excerpt:
## Description
The PR hardens TLS 1.3 EncryptedExtensions parsing on the client side. The parser now verifies that it consumes the full `buf..end` range after processing the internal extensions vector, and rejects messages with trailing bytes by sending `decode_error` and returning `MBEDTLS_ERR_SSL_DECODE_ERROR`.

## PR checklist
- [ ] **changelog** not required because: this is a small bug fix with no API change
- [ ] **framework PR** not required
- [ ] **TF-PSA-Crypto development PR** not required because: no TF-PSA-Crypto code was changed
- [ ] **TF-PSA-Crypto 1.1 PR** not required because: no TF-PSA-Crypto code was changed
- [ ] **mbedtls development PR** not required because: this PR is itself the development PR
- [ ] **mbedtls 4.1 PR** not required because: backport not prepared yet
- [ ] **mbedtls 3.6 PR** not required because: backport not prepared yet
- **tests**  provided
title: ssl: reject trailing bytes in TLS 1.3 encrypted extensions

body_excerpt:
## Description

Backport of #10715 to the `mbedtls-4.1` branch.

The deserialization paths for TLS 1.3 sessions and SSL contexts have been
hardened to reject malformed input as a second line of defence (the primary
protection remains application-side integrity protection of serialized data):

* `ssl_tls13_session_load` now rejects ALPN and hostname fields that are not
  null-terminated within the announced length, and rejects buffers that have
  unconsumed trailing data after the full record.
* `ssl_context_load` now rejects serialized SSL contexts whose DTLS Connection
  ID length exceeds the maximum supported size, instead of overflowing the
  internal buffer.

New tests cover all four error paths.

Related: CVE-2026-34877 (mbedtls-security-advisory-2026-03-serialized-data); see #10682, #10683, #10715.

## PR checklist

- [x] **changelog** provided
- [x] **development PR** provided #10715
- [x] **3.6 PR** provided #10762
- [x] **4.1 PR** provided here
- [x] **tests** provided
title: Backport to 4.1: harden serialized data deserialization

body_excerpt:
## Description

Backport of #10715 to the `mbedtls-3.6` branch.

The deserialization paths for TLS 1.3 sessions and SSL contexts have been
hardened to reject malformed input as a second line of defence (the primary
protection remains application-side integrity protection of serialized data):

* `ssl_tls13_session_load` now rejects ALPN and hostname fields that are not
  null-terminated within the announced length, and rejects buffers that have
  unconsumed trailing data after the full record.
* `ssl_context_load` now rejects serialized SSL contexts whose DTLS Connection
  ID length exceeds the maximum supported size, instead of overflowing the
  internal buffer.

The TLS 1.3 session-load tests from #10715 are included. The dedicated
CID-length test relies on test helpers introduced after 3.6
(`mbedtls_test_ssl_dtls_join_endpoints`, `mbedtls_test_ssl_perform_connection`)
and is omitted from this backport. The CID bounds check itself is unchanged.

This supersedes #10683.

Related: CVE-2026-34877 (mbedtls-security-advisory-2026-03-serialized-data); see #10682, #10715, #10761.

## PR checklist

- [x] **changelog** provided
- [x] **development PR** provided #10715
- [x] **3.6 PR** provided here (supersedes #10683)
- [x] **4.1 PR** provided #10761
- [x] **tests** provided (TLS 1.3 session-load mutation tests; CID test omitted, see description)
title: Backport to 3.6: harden serialized data deserialization
